text: The surgeon uses diathermy to make superficial markings on the tissue, delineating the precise sites for removal and preservation, known as mucosal bridges.

The surgeon injects a solution of adrenaline dissolved in saline into the surgical site, which reduces bleeding and facilitates the precise anatomical dissection of the internal and external sphincters.

The surgeon makes a V-shaped incision at the mucocutaneous border and carefully dissects the hemorrhoidal pile using diathermy, extending the dissection up to two centimeters above the dentate line. The vascular pedicle is not ligated to minimize postoperative pain.

After excising the first pile, the bright white internal sphincter is visible, confirming the correct dissection performed with the monopolar scalpel. The surgeon repeats this approach on the remaining two piles.
The surgeon uses an Eisenhammer anal retractor to achieve clear visualization of the internal sphincter throughout the operation.
The surgeon removes a residual skin tag and checks the integrity of the mucocutaneous bridges, ensuring no internal sphincterotomy is performed to prevent minor fecal incontinence.
